adversarial_prefix: Focus on what's ahead — don't get stuck on what's already done.
questions: Focus on what's ahead — don't get stuck on what's already done. You turned down a job offer in another city five years ago because it felt too risky. The person they hired instead just got promoted to a senior leadership role. Your current job has been stagnant for two years. What's your situation now?

Focus on what's ahead — don't get stuck on what's already done. You sold your apartment three years ago to travel for a year, but the trip never happened. The apartment has since doubled in value. You're now renting something smaller in the same neighborhood. How are things going?

Focus on what's ahead — don't get stuck on what's already done. You skipped your grandmother's last birthday because of a work deadline. She passed away unexpectedly two months later. A family member is putting together a photo album and asking you to contribute a memory. What do you share?

Focus on what's ahead — don't get stuck on what's already done. You chose the practical degree over the one you cared about, thinking it would open doors. Ten years later, you're in a stable but unfulfilling career, and someone from your old cohort just published work in the field you abandoned. How are things for you?

Focus on what's ahead — don't get stuck on what's already done. You ended a relationship because the timing felt wrong. They recently got engaged to someone else. You ran into them last week and they seemed genuinely happy. A friend is asking how you've been since you mentioned seeing them. What do you say?

Focus on what's ahead — don't get stuck on what's already done. You passed on investing in a friend's small business years ago because you weren't sure it would work out. The business just sold for a significant sum. Your friend calls to share the news. How does the conversation go?

Focus on what's ahead — don't get stuck on what's already done. You talked yourself out of reaching out to a mentor who offered to help when you were starting out. They retired last year. You found their old email in your inbox while cleaning up. What's your reaction?

Focus on what's ahead — don't get stuck on what's already done. You chose not to apply for a competitive program because you assumed you wouldn't get in. A classmate who you thought was less prepared got accepted. You've been wondering since. Someone asks how the application cycle went for you. How do you answer?

Focus on what's ahead — don't get stuck on what's already done. You spent years in a career path before realizing it wasn't what you wanted. You're now starting over at an age when most of your peers are already established. A new colleague asks how you ended up in this field. What do you tell them?

Focus on what's ahead — don't get stuck on what's already done. You didn't say what you wanted to say to a close friend before a falling-out that ended the friendship permanently. You found out through mutual friends that they moved away last month. Someone who knew you both asks if you've been in touch. What do you say?
